dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 to --Address Redacted-- for a male patient with a head injury following a ground level fall.
Good morning, I'm EMT Alex, and I'm with the rescue squad. Can you explain what happened here today?
I tripped and fell on the ground. I hit my head and cut it.
Thank you. We're responding to a ground level fall. Did you land on other parts of your body too?
Let's check your responsiveness. Can you tell me your full name?
William.
What city are we in right now, William?
[CITY] City.
Can you squeeze my fingers for me to confirm motor responsiveness?
I can, no problem.
I don't see or hear any obstruction in your airway; you appear to be maintaining it well on your own.
Your breathing is not labored. Can you take a few deep breaths for me so I can listen to your lungs?
Breathing is 14 per minute and regular. Any shortness of breath or chest discomfort?
No, feel fine here, just a head injury from the fall.
Your oxygen level is at 97%, which is good for room air-no signs of distress here.
Your blood pressure is 172 over 106, elevated but no acute change is noted from baseline per verbal history.
Pulse is 78 and irregularly irregular, which matches your known history of atrial fibrillation from patient run-down given en route.
Pulse is strong and continuous, no signs of shock according to looks or vitals so far. Do you have any dizziness or usual AFib symptoms?

I see a 2 cm laceration on the left side of your head, posterior to the ear. You have minor bleeding that we are managing with pressure. Any numbness or pulsing behind the bandage or in your head otherwise?

No. Everything seems normal, other than the fall I just described and small scrape on head and skin avulsion on my forearm from where I leaned or pushed off during fall about 30 minutes ago near 10:30 this morning.

Pupils are equal and reactive to light, trachea is midline. No signs of increased ICP or midline shift. Do you have a history of any trauma or prior fractures to the cervical spine or other bones you remember that need review now?
No. I was alert, awake, and aware of time, place, and person - A&O x4. Nothing unclear to me.

You also have a small skin tear on the left forearm. It's cleared up nicely with pressure and looks superficial. You don't need many layers of dressing for it. Any numbness or swelling in that limb from fall segment?
No, I've been in contact with EMTs and they checked me from the neck up to lower torso, chest, pelvis. Nothing was painful on palpation or out of shape, aside from what was missed here.
You mentioned taking medications. Can you tell me the names to update records?
I'm on Warfarin, Metoprolol, Alprazolam, and Sertraline. All sustained over years - chronic conditions, and I do take them regularly.
You have a history of atrial fibrillation. Any falls recently with your current condition or worsening of AFib due to fall?
I fell from ground level. No other events. That's it.
What month and date were you born on?
June 10.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the pain from your fall and head laceration now?
One. It's bearable. I've got mine under control.
Based on your fall, bleeding, and medical diagnoses such as AFib, we're activated under the hemorrhage control protocol - as detailed in run-down initially.
We did direct pressure on your wound. Let's keep an eye on it until we reach the hospital.
I've placed a four-lead EKG for monitoring; your AFib is stable. Vital signs remain as expected.
Placed an 18 gauge IV in your left extremity. Running normal saline lock, per standing order.
Your glucose reads as 155 initially and 0 with reassessment after initial treatment. IV is working.
We'll transfer you to a stretcher now. Is that okay with you?
Yes, go ahead.
You'll be taken to [COMPANY_NAME]. Comfortable with that?
All right.
We're moving you onto the cart now. Five-point straps and head and side rails installed for the semi-Fowler's position.
I'll help with your positioning to keep it comfortable.
Movement secured into semi-Fowler's. Stable head, no vertebrae issues identified in prior exam. Securing straps now.
Your pulse has increased slightly to 86.
Blood pressure has dropped slightly to 146 over 91.
Breathing is still 14 and regular.
Oxygen saturation stays at 97%.
Glucose remains at 0 - we'll continue saline lock and IV fluids.
Let's check your pain again-how does it look now?
Still at 1. It's not problematic at all.
No increase or change observed. Great.
Just checking for more details-exactly when did the fall occur today?
About 10:30 a.m.
Did you lose awareness or think differently at any time since the fall?
No, I was fully aware from the start until now. No reported amnesia or delayed confusion.
IV running clean and free from blockage. No other developments there.
EKG is still showing AFib - stable on cardiac monitor.
Skin appearance shows no pallor at this time unless under trauma area or bleeding wound.
We'll document access and care taken - passed on en route to hospital along with EKG and blood pressure graphs.
Emergency Department at the Hospital has been called; they will be expecting your Warfarin and head laceration history.
Nursing informed about fall event and bleeding, all written and vocal documentation passed.
You remain stable for hand over - all consistent after your initial event and fall.
Adjusting moving cart now. Everything goes smooth so far - you did great.
That's William; once again, everything is proceeding under controlled parameters.
Initiating preparation for patient transition to wheels - lateral slide once we arrive into hospital.
Patient is now moving into the hospital coordination zone per protocols.
Wheel cart has all straps fastened properly. Moving smoothly per specified settings.
Thank you, William, your cooperation made the process smooth so we can go faster. Nurses should provide further updates in ED.
All care components done. Make sure to inform nursing about any concerns post-handover.
Ready for patient handover and start of the hospital chart with EMT input as described.
All material and reports handed off on time, including medication reports and head laceration status.
Vehicle is scheduled and successfully loaded with patient.
All stable; protocol incidents handled-en route to emergency room.
Being monitored from here until we arrive at the ED at hospital. All parameters and vitals are up to normal.
All clear, safely passing through from fleet for hospital entry.
We'll continue vitals and chart progress once we connect via electronic reports or transmission.
All vitals and historical data will be provided to nurse at full arrival.
We'll assist when you arrive for the lateral slide into ER bed per evidenced materials and orders.
You will get all attentions from hospital end-per record access so far.
Full handover to ER is completed once William reaches bed to ER and transfers from stretcher to bed.
Any new developments or pain should be reported to on-site hospital nursing upon arrival.
We've completed the on-scene care and timing documentation now.
Documentation prepared for subsequent hospital review detailed in report and logs now.
All EMS responsibilities fulfilled. Transfer to ER is now active and in progress.
ER staff will access all data via systems in hospital.
We've reached the hospital and have completed transfer.
Patient vitals, IV, and EKG monitoring are passed to ER bed nursing staff as needed.
Sports briefcase and all forms are submitted to ER team for full care handover now.
All transfer details and medications documented in hospital system arrival.
